name: Galway International Oyster & Seafood Festival
tagline: The world's oldest oyster festival, where the first native flat oysters of the season are shucked, championed and washed down with Guinness on Galway's Atlantic edge.
dates: 25–27 September 2026 (72nd edition, confirmed); 2027 edition expected last weekend of September — 24–26 September 2027 (expected)
history: The festival was born in 1954 out of a hotelier's off-season gamble: Brian Collins, manager of Galway's Great Southern Hotel (today the Hotel Meyrick), wanted to lure visitors to the west of Ireland in a quiet month and hit on the idea of celebrating the opening of the native oyster season, which runs September to April. The inaugural gathering drew just 34 attendees. The premise was simple and remains unchanged: the Galway native flat oyster (Ostrea edulis), harvested from the beds of inner Galway Bay around Clarinbridge and Kilcolgan, is at its briny best just as the season opens — reason enough for a party.

From those modest beginnings it grew into what is often called the most internationally recognised Irish festival after St Patrick's Day. For decades events were split between the oyster villages south of the city and the Great Southern's ballroom, before consolidating in Galway city by the mid-1980s under a famous red-and-white striped marquee at the Spanish Arch. The festival's competitive heart arrived with the shucking contests: the Irish Oyster Opening Championship on Friday evening and, on Saturday, the World Oyster Opening Championship — the 'Oyster Olympics' — in which national champions race to open 30 oysters, judged on speed and presentation. Accolades piled up: named one of Europe's Seven Best Festivals in 1987, and in 2000 The Sunday Times counted it among the '12 greatest shows on earth'.

The event has weathered venue wanderings — the Radisson in 2009, a marquee at the Docks in 2011 (when it took its current name, the Galway International Oyster & Seafood Festival) — before returning for its 60th anniversary in 2014 to the City Fishmarket beside the Spanish Arch, where the festival Big Top now stands. Beyond the marquee, the weekend has become a city-wide celebration of Galway's food culture: tastings, a seafood trail through the Latin Quarter's pubs, cooking demonstrations, the Mardi Gras party and a black-tie(ish) Saturday gala, all soundtracked by trad sessions. It is both a serious shellfish showcase and one of Ireland's great excuses for craic.
whyGo: Nowhere else can you eat native European flat oysters this fresh, in the season's opening week, yards from the bay they grew in — a rarer and wilder-tasting thing than the rock oysters served everywhere else. Add the theatre of the World Oyster Opening Championships, Guinness by the crate, and Galway's pubs in full late-September voice, and it's a compact, high-spirited weekend that punches far above its size.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Dublin (DUB) with Aer Lingus (direct, ~2h20), then Citylink or GoBus/Aircoach coach direct from Dublin Airport to Galway city
gettingThere.notes: The coach leaves from outside Dublin Airport arrivals and drops in central Galway — no transfer into Dublin needed. Book flights ~3 months out; the festival weekend is popular.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Dublin (DUB), Dublin Airport → Heuston Station (Dublin Express/747 bus, ~45min), then Irish Rail InterCity Heuston → Galway Ceannt (~2h30)
gettingThere.notes: The train is the scenic, comfortable option across the midlands; book on irishrail.ie for the cheapest fares. Galway Ceannt station is a 5-minute walk from the festival marquee.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Shannon (SNN) via London Heathrow or Dublin (Aer Lingus/British Airways one-stop), then Citylink or Bus Éireann coach Shannon → Galway (~1h30)
gettingThere.notes: No direct ZRH–Shannon service, so this only wins if fares align — but Shannon is much closer to Galway and quieter to transit. Useful if pairing the trip with the Cliffs of Moher or the Burren.

costs.notes: Estimates for flights from Zürich, 2–3 nights and festival entry. Accommodation is the swing factor — Galway is small and hotels double their rates and sell out for the festival weekend, so book by early summer. Festival Big Top sessions (Sat/Sun afternoon tastings with the championships) run roughly €60–100 per person including seafood; the Saturday gala evening with dinner and bands is €150+. Thrifty means hostel/B&B outside the centre and à-la-carte oysters in pubs; splurge means a Latin Quarter hotel, gala tickets and a Michelin-starred dinner.

weather: Late September in Galway means 10–15°C, brisk Atlantic winds and a good chance of sideways showers between bright spells. Pack a proper waterproof shell, layers and shoes that can handle wet cobbles; bring something smart (jacket, or black tie if you're doing the gala) for the evening events — the marquee is heated but the walk there isn't.
tips: Book accommodation the moment you commit — Galway's city-centre beds sell out months ahead for festival weekend and rates spike; Salthill (a 20-minute seafront walk) is the best-value fallback.
Buy Big Top session tickets early and aim for Saturday afternoon: that's when the World Oyster Opening Championship happens, and it's the festival at full tilt.

Native flat oysters taste stronger and more mineral than the rock oysters you may know — try them unadorned first, no lemon, with a half of Guinness rather than a full pint so you can pace a long day.
Friday evening (Irish Opening Championship and the festival opening) is cheaper and looser than Saturday; Sunday is the mellow, family-friendly seafood-trail day — a good arrival/departure buffer.

You don't need a ticket to enjoy the weekend: the Latin Quarter's pubs (Tigh Neachtain, The Quays) run seafood specials and trad sessions all weekend — but restaurant tables need reservations weeks ahead.
Take the direct Citylink/GoBus coach from Dublin Airport rather than crossing Dublin to Heuston station — it saves an hour and a taxi, and drops you five minutes from the Spanish Arch.

localRecs.name: Moran's Oyster Cottage
localRecs.note: Thatched, seventh-generation oyster house on the weir at Kilcolgan, beside the native oyster beds themselves — a 25-minute taxi south and the definitive pre- or post-festival pilgrimage.

localRecs.name: Tigh Neachtain
localRecs.note: Blue-fronted 1894 pub at the heart of the Latin Quarter; snug corners, whiskey depth, and festival-weekend trad sessions. The unofficial living room of Galway.

localRecs.name: McDonagh's
localRecs.note: Quay Street institution for fish and chips and a proper seafood counter — the fast, cheap, excellent counterweight to marquee tastings.

localRecs.name: Aniar
localRecs.note: Michelin-starred temple of west-of-Ireland terroir tasting menus — book well ahead if you want the splurge dinner of the trip.

localRecs.name: The Long Walk & Spanish Arch
localRecs.note: The postcard row of coloured houses along the Corrib estuary, right beside the festival marquee — walk it at golden hour with the swans and the Claddagh across the water.

photos.caption: Competitors race the clock at the World Oyster Opening Championship, the 'Oyster Olympics' at the heart of the festival's Saturday programme.
Galway native flat oysters — Ostrea edulis from the beds of inner Galway Bay — shucked and served at a festival stall.
The Spanish Arch and the coloured houses of the Long Walk, beside the City Fishmarket site where the festival Big Top is raised.
